questions.text: On which date did the storming of the Bastille occur?
questions.explanation: On the morning of 14 July 1789, the city of Paris was in a state of alarm, and the people stormed the fortress-prison, the Bastille.

questions.text: Who was the King of France during the outbreak of the Revolution?
questions.explanation: In 1774, Louis XVI of the Bourbon family ascended the throne of France.

questions.text: The French society was divided into how many estates?
questions.explanation: French society in the eighteenth century was divided into three estates: the Clergy, the Nobility, and the Third Estate.

questions.text: Which estate paid all the taxes in pre-revolutionary France?
questions.explanation: Only members of the third estate paid taxes; the first two estates enjoyed privileges by birth.

questions.text: What was the 'Tithe'?
options: A tax levied by the Church
A tax paid directly to the state
questions.explanation: Tithe was a tax levied by the church, comprising one-tenth of the agricultural produce.

questions.text: The direct tax paid to the state was called:
questions.explanation: Taille was a direct tax to be paid to the state by members of the Third Estate.

questions.text: Who wrote the book 'The Two Treatises of Government'?
questions.explanation: In his 'Two Treatises of Government', John Locke sought to refute the doctrine of the divine and absolute right of the monarch.

questions.text: Who proposed a division of power within the government in 'The Spirit of the Laws'?
questions.explanation: Montesquieu proposed a division of power between the legislative, the executive, and the judiciary.

questions.text: When did the Estates-General meeting begin at Versailles?
questions.explanation: On 5 May 1789, Louis XVI called together an assembly of the Estates-General to pass proposals for new taxes.

questions.text: Where did the representatives of the Third Estate assemble on 20 June 1789?
questions.explanation: On 20 June, they assembled in the hall of an indoor tennis court in the grounds of Versailles and declared themselves a National Assembly.

questions.text: What was the main objective of the National Assembly's draft constitution of 1791?
options: To limit the powers of the monarch
To start a war with Austria
questions.explanation: Its main object was to limit the powers of the monarch by assigning them to different institutions.

questions.text: Who were 'Active Citizens' in the 1791 Constitution?
options: Only men above 25 who paid taxes
questions.explanation: Only men above 25 years of age who paid taxes equal to at least 3 days of a labourer's wage were given the right to vote.

questions.text: What does the 'Red Phrygian Cap' symbolize?
questions.explanation: The red cap was worn by slaves upon becoming free, symbolizing liberty.

questions.text: The patriotic song 'Marseillaise' was composed by:
questions.explanation: It was composed by the poet Roget de L'Isle and is now the national anthem of France.

questions.text: The members of the Jacobin club were known as:
questions.explanation: They were known as sans-culottes, meaning 'those without knee breeches'.

questions.text: When was France declared a Republic?
questions.explanation: On 21 September 1792, the newly elected assembly, the Convention, abolished the monarchy and declared France a republic.

questions.text: On what charge was Louis XVI executed?
questions.explanation: Louis XVI was sentenced to death by a court on the charge of treason.

questions.text: The period from 1793 to 1794 is referred to as the:
questions.explanation: This period is called the Reign of Terror due to Robespierre’s policy of severe control and punishment.

questions.text: Which device was used for execution during the Reign of Terror?
questions.explanation: The guillotine is a device consisting of two poles and a blade with which a person is beheaded.

questions.text: Which of these laws was NOT introduced by Robespierre's government?
options: Use of expensive white flour was forbidden
questions.explanation: While the Convention abolished slavery in 1794, Robespierre's focus was on social equality; Napoleon reintroduced it later.

questions.text: After the fall of the Jacobin government, power was seized by:
questions.explanation: The fall of Robespierre allowed the wealthier middle classes to seize power and introduce a new constitution.

questions.text: The Directory was an executive made up of how many members?
questions.explanation: The Directory was an executive made up of five members, intended as a safeguard against one-man rule.

questions.text: In which year did women in France finally win the right to vote?
questions.explanation: It was finally in 1946 that women in France won the right to vote after a long struggle.

questions.text: The most important of the women's political clubs was the:
options: Society of Revolutionary and Republican Women
questions.explanation: This was the most famous of about sixty women's political clubs that came up in French cities.

questions.text: The triangular slave trade was between Europe, Africa and:
questions.explanation: The slave trade involved a triangular route between Europe, Africa, and the Americas.

questions.text: Slavery was finally abolished in French colonies in:
questions.explanation: Slavery was finally abolished in French colonies in 1848.

questions.text: Who crowned himself Emperor of France in 1804?
questions.explanation: In 1804, Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of France and set out to conquer Europe.

questions.text: Napoleon was finally defeated at the Battle of ______ in 1815.
questions.explanation: Napoleon’s career ended with his final defeat at Waterloo in 1815.

questions.text: Which of these was a legacy of the French Revolution?
questions.explanation: The ideas of liberty and democratic rights were the most important legacy of the French Revolution.

questions.text: Who among the following Indian individuals responded to the ideas coming from revolutionary France?
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan and Rammohan Roy are two examples of individuals who responded to the ideas of the French Revolution.

questions.text: What was the unit of currency in France until 1794?
questions.explanation: Livre was the currency of France, discontinued in 1794.

questions.text: The term 'Old Regime' is used to describe the society and institutions of France before:
questions.explanation: The term Old Regime is usually used to describe the French society and institutions before the 1789 revolution.

questions.text: The population of France rose from 23 million in 1715 to ______ in 1789.
questions.explanation: This rapid increase in population led to a rapid increase in the demand for foodgrains.

questions.text: A 'subsistence crisis' occurs when:
options: The basic means of livelihood are endangered
questions.explanation: It is an extreme situation where the basic means of livelihood are endangered, often due to bad harvest.

questions.text: Who proposed the model of a social contract between people and their representatives?
questions.explanation: Rousseau carried the idea forward, proposing a form of government based on a social contract.

questions.text: Which estate representatives declared themselves as the 'National Assembly'?
questions.explanation: The Third Estate, representing the majority, declared themselves the National Assembly of France.

questions.text: The National Assembly completed the draft of the constitution in:
questions.explanation: The National Assembly completed the draft of the constitution in 1791.

questions.text: Who was the leader of the Jacobin club?
questions.explanation: Maximilien Robespierre was the prominent leader of the Jacobins.

questions.text: What happened to the bastille prisoners?
questions.explanation: In the storming of Bastille, the commander was killed and the 7 prisoners were released.

questions.text: The word 'Livres' stands for:
questions.explanation: Livre was the unit of currency in France.
